'''Edward Kilham''', also known as '''Ed Kilham''', is a [[Wikipedia:Video game developer|video game developer]] whose work includes ''[[Star Wars: X-Wing (video game)|Star Wars: X-Wing]]'' and [[Star Wars: TIE Fighter (video game)|''Star Wars: TIE Fighter'']].

==Biography==
[[File:X-wingTeam.jpg|thumb|left|250px|Edward Kilham (left) with [[Rusel DeMaria]] (center) and [[Lawrence Holland]] (right)]]
Edward "Killer" Kilham<ref name="XW:OSG">''[[X-Wing: The Official Strategy Guide]]''</ref> started creating [[Star Wars video games|video games]] and graphics systems for the [[Wikipedia:Apple II series|Apple II]] [[Wikipedia:Personal computer|personal computer]] while he was at [[Wikipedia:High school|high school]] and moved on to the [[Wikipedia:Amiga|Amiga]] in [[1985]], for which he wrote a color font editing utility called ''The Calligrapher''. He studied [[Wikipedia:Computer science|Computer Science]] at the [[Wikipedia:University of Oregon|University of Oregon]], [[Wikipedia:Graduation|graduating]] with a [[Wikipedia:Latin honors|Magna Cum Laude]] in [[1990]].<ref name="XW:OSG" />

While still at [[Wikipedia:University|university]], Kilham worked on [[Wikipedia:RoboSport|''RoboSport'']] for [[Wikipedia:Maxis|Maxis]], and he joined [[LucasArts]] after graduating. He began developing a cinematic engine and officially joined [[Lawrence Holland]]'s [[Wikipedia:Simulation|simulator]] team, which later became [[Totally Games]], in [[1991]], combining his cinematic approach with Holland's simulator technology.<ref name="XW:OSG" /> The pair worked together on [[1993]]'s ''Star Wars: X-Wing'' and its [[1994]] sequel, ''Star Wars: TIE Fighter''.
